questions.question: Why is a direct RPC call to a central flag service on every request usually the wrong architecture for flag evaluation?
options: It adds 1-5ms of network latency to every request and makes the flag service a hard dependency on every service's hot path.
RPC calls cannot carry the user context needed for targeting rule evaluation.
Central flag services cannot handle more than 10K concurrent connections.
Flag evaluations require distributed transactions that are incompatible with RPC.
questions.explanation: At 250M evaluations/second platform-wide, any network call per evaluation is economically and latency-infeasible. In-process evaluation against a local state copy is the only viable approach.

questions.question: Why is consistent hashing (hash(flag_key + user_id)) preferred over random assignment for percentage rollouts?
options: It ensures the same user always sees the same variant for the same flag, providing a stable user experience without needing a database lookup or sticky session.
It distributes users evenly across database shards, improving query performance.
It prevents any single user from being assigned to more than one experiment.
It makes it possible to target flags at specific geographic regions.
questions.explanation: Deterministic hashing gives stable assignments: the same (flag_key, user_id) pair always produces the same bucket, so a user's experience is consistent across requests and services.

questions.question: The flag streaming service is unavailable for 5 minutes. What should SDK instances do during this outage?
options: Continue evaluating flags using the last-known in-memory flag state; log staleness duration; alert if staleness exceeds the configured threshold.
Disable all flag-gated features and serve default behavior until the streaming service recovers.
Make direct database queries to the flag store for each evaluation during the outage.
Pause request processing until the flag streaming service is restored.
questions.explanation: Stale-but-working is the correct degraded mode. Disabling features or blocking requests during a flag service outage is worse than serving slightly stale flag state.

questions.question: A propagation bus drops one flag_changed event. Flag state diverges across 50K SDK instances. What is the reconciliation mechanism?
options: SDK instances periodically fetch a full flag snapshot from the control plane and compare it with local state, correcting any divergence.
The propagation bus replays all events in order every minute to ensure consistency.
The flag store sends a hash of its full state to all SDKs; any mismatch triggers a full reload.
Engineers manually restart SDK instances when flag divergence is detected.
questions.explanation: Periodic full snapshot reconciliation catches any missed events. The snapshot fetch interval (e.g., 5 minutes) is the upper bound on how long a missed update causes divergence.

questions.question: A kill switch must propagate to all 50K SDK instances within 10 seconds. How does the system prioritize this over normal flag updates?
options: Kill switch events are published to a dedicated high-priority topic and delivered via a priority path in the propagation bus that SDKs process before other pending updates.
Kill switches are applied by restarting all service instances, which forces them to reload flag state.
Kill switch propagation uses the same mechanism as normal flags but sets a shorter polling interval.
Kill switches are evaluated at the load balancer level, bypassing the SDK entirely.
questions.explanation: A dedicated priority path for kill switches ensures that the most urgent operational action is not queued behind low-priority flag changes in the propagation pipeline.

questions.question: An engineer wants to target a flag at users whose current request comes from a specific country. Why is this harder than targeting by user_id?
options: Country is a request-level attribute that changes per request and cannot be stored in the static user context baked into the local flag state; the SDK must accept it as a dynamic input.
Country targeting requires a database lookup that makes evaluation slower than the 1ms SLO.
Country attributes are not supported by consistent hashing, so percentage rollouts cannot be combined with country targeting.
Country detection requires geolocation APIs that cannot run in-process.
questions.explanation: User_id-based targeting is static; country-based targeting requires the request context to be passed into the evaluate call dynamically. The SDK must accept rich context, not just a user identifier.

questions.question: Which observability signal best detects that flag state is silently stale on a subset of SDK instances?
options: SDK instance staleness metric — time since last update received — reported as a histogram and alerted when p99 exceeds the propagation SLO.
Number of flag evaluations per second across all instances.
Total number of flags in the system.
Flag change audit log volume per hour.
questions.explanation: Per-instance staleness directly measures whether the propagation mechanism is keeping instances up to date. Global evaluation counts do not reveal divergence between instances.

questions.question: What is the trade-off of using push-based propagation (WebSocket/SSE) compared to polling at a fixed interval?
options: Push delivers flag changes with seconds of latency but requires maintaining 50K persistent connections; polling is simpler but guarantees staleness equal to the polling interval.
Push is less reliable than polling because WebSocket connections drop frequently.
Polling at 1-second intervals achieves the same latency as push with no additional infrastructure.
Push propagation requires a distributed transaction to ensure all instances update atomically.
questions.explanation: Push's low latency advantage comes with the operational cost of managing tens of thousands of persistent connections. Polling's simplicity comes at the cost of guaranteed staleness proportional to the poll interval.
